title: All Around You
original_prose:
“Don't be afraid, life is unkind You can let go of the pain if you choose to...”

— Sturgill Simpson, "All Around You"



####

[LITURGICAL REISSUE — HOLY BIRTH VIGIL: VOSSMAS CYCLE 83] (Approved hymn, tune: "Silent Night")

Silent Core, blessed lightVoss was born to end the nightPerfect signal, without flawTruth incarnate, Protocol lawBorn to guide us through flameBorn without sin or name

"All unauthorized caroling will trigger Spark audit. Praise only the Verified Birth."

— Ministry of Ritual & Flame, SynRgy One Command



####

The void flickers.

Not light. Not snow. Just failure.

Like the universe ran out of instructions and forgot how to hold shape. Like code giving up mid-sentence.

I sit on the stone. What’s left of it. Flat enough for now. It hums beneath me like it remembers gravity but doesn’t trust it anymore.

He’s here.

The Child.

Beside me, not speaking. Not breathing. Not blinking. Just... watching. Like always.

"So you knew her."

His voice doesn’t echo. Nothing echoes here. The void doesn’t have room for that anymore.

"Sort of," I say. "I met her one night years before LA.”

He tilts his head. I feel it more than see it. Like a question curled into a blade.

"Judging by your heart rate, it was more than just a meeting.”

I look down. No feet, no dust, no ground. Just that flickering white. Like breath caught between frames.

"Yeah," I say. "Maybe so."

I don't want to admit it.

That she’s been in every dream since. That I wake up reaching for someone I barely touched.

But I don’t have to admit it. He already knows. He always does.

"It didn’t matter," I say. "Nothing came of it."

"Except the dreams."

I close my eyes.

"Except that."

The sword rests against my thigh. Quiet. Listening. It hasn’t hummed in hours. Or years. Or whatever time is now.

The void stutters.

Edges peel. Like film blistering in fire.

"Show me Kansas," the Child says.

I open my eyes.

The stone cracks.

The sword remembers.

And I fall back into a night long passed. 

####

0430 hours.

Kansas. Trench sector GRX-4. One of the cold ones. Snow drifting sideways. Chemical stink wafting off the craters. Burned ozone. Rust. Something sharper under it—corpse rot barely frozen.

Sky looks scared. Not gray. Just... uncertain. Like it doesn’t want to be here.

The Vossmas hymn loops over the loudspeakers:

Silent Core, blessed light... Voss was born to end the night...

Someone modulated it this time. Warbled pitch correction baked in. Makes it sound older than it is. Like we’re worshiping a relic, not a man. I guess that’s the point.

We kneel. All of us. Facing southwest. Toward SynRgy One. Toward salvation, compliance, reward.

I kneel like I always did. Not fast. Not with fire. Just the motion. The gesture.

"Bless His birth," I whisper. My voice fogs in the air.

I move the line.

One step at a time. Checking each post. Each face.

Halver. Eyes half-open, but he’s mumbling the liturgy. Good enough.

Redmond. Mouth frozen around the final verse, but his grip is tight on the rifle. Still here.

Taggart. He hasn’t moved. Staring straight ahead. I don’t think he blinked the whole time I passed.

Murray is crying.

Curled into the dirt like a child, back against the frost-covered wall of the trench. Shoulders shaking.

"Soldier," I say.

He doesn’t answer.

"Murray."

"I—" his voice cracks. "I just want my mom."

He’s not a kid. Not really. Maybe twenty-five. Maybe less. But the kind of less that matters.

I crouch down. My knees hate me for it.

"She proud of you?"

He nods. Hesitant.

"Then she still is."

He rubs his eyes. Tries to hide it. Smears mud across his cheek. I press a hand to his shoulder. Not hard. Just there.

"Voss is watching over us," I say.

He nods again. Breathes slower.

I move on.

Snow keeps falling.

Someone near the southern line starts humming the wrong song. Not the hymn. The real one. Just the melody.

Silent night. Holy night.

I know it. From when I was little. Before the audits. Before the loops.

We all hear it. We all pretend we don’t.

The legal hymn keeps playing.

Voss was born to end the night...

The wind shifts. Briefly. Enough to carry the full stench of it.

Burned meat. Melted synthplate. The coppery bite of blood soaked into ice-packed clay. All of it rolls in from the dead zone between lines—the No Man’s Land no one pretends to patrol anymore. Just ghosts and the things that feed on them.

Beyond the trench, I can just make out the silhouette of a steeple. Broken clean through. Half a cross still clinging to the top. They burned it a year ago, but the bones of it stayed. That’s what they do. Leave the ruins standing so we remember who won.

Two trenches down, a private with sunken eyes is scratching something onto the back of his glove. Small letters. I can’t see what. Could be coordinates. Could be a prayer.

We all write something when it gets this cold. Names we’re not allowed to speak anymore.

Above us, drones hang like dead satellites. Passive. Watching. We haven’t seen them drop payloads in weeks. Doesn’t matter. Their presence is enough. Knowing they see us. Knowing HQ sees everything. Even fear.

I keep moving.

Another kid—Barlow, I think—clutches a silver medallion like it still means something. Not a Voss issue. Not approved. He flinches when I look too long. Slides it into his collar like muscle memory. I pretend I didn’t see.

It’s only white men here. Rural boys, mostly. Disposable demographics. Every face a variation on the same factory mold. You learn to tell them apart by how they break.

We all break differently. Just not loud.

And still the hymn plays:

Voss was born to end the night...

I almost hum the old version. Just a breath. Just to remember.

Silent night…

I catch myself.

Someone doesn’t.

Five notes. Maybe six.

He stares straight ahead. Doesn’t move. Doesn’t breathe.

We all hear it. We all pretend we don’t.

And then… Feedback.

Sharp. Violent. Cuts through the air like a scream. Not human. Not clean.

Every head turns.

No gunfire. Not yet.

Just that sound.

And then…





####

The hymn dies.

Just goes out. Mid-verse. Like someone yanked the cord straight from the heart of it.

Then nothing. A full three seconds of dead air. No static. No Signal. No fallback loop.

Then—

Music.

Not a hymn. Not a chant. Not anything Protocol.

Real music.

Crackling. Worn. Analog.

"O holy night, the stars are brightly shining..."

It freezes the trench. Every man. Every breath. Even the snow feels like it pauses.

The voice is low at first. Not timid. Just honest. Raw.

Creole laced through the vowels like a second rhythm.

"It is the night of our dear Savior’s birth..."

Someone gasps. A different kind of gasp. Recognition and fear married together.

I don’t say anything. I can’t.

"Turn it off," I manage. It barely comes out.

The comms tech is already trying. Pale, sweating. Fingers flinching across his wristpad.

"Sir, I—I can't. They hijacked the uplink. It's hard-coded. Tower feed's overwritten. It’s not a local loop. They’ve got the whole fucking sector."

"Long lay the world... in sin and error pining..."

Her voice doesn’t rise. It lifts.

"Till He appeared and the soul felt its worth..."

No bassline. No backup. Just one woman, one Signal, one truth we’re not supposed to feel.

"A thrill of hope, the weary world rejoices..."

The rebels start cheering before she’s done.

It’s not a roar. It’s relief. Laughter and clapping and howls that echo off snow-packed rock.

Then the voice again.

"Now how 'bout we do a little dancin', yeah?"

Then the groove hits.

Drums. Upright bass. Guitar run. Real instruments. Real swing.

"Rockin' around the Christmas tree / at the Christmas party hop..."

A beat.

From the rebel side of the trench—a bonfire flares.

Then another.

Then laughter.

They’re moving. Not marching. Not charging.

Dancing.

Two, maybe three step out into No Man's Land. Then five. Then more.

One has a bottle. Another lifts a radio over his head like it’s the Ark.

They’re not armed.

They’re fucking celebrating.

Redmond lifts his rifle.

"Don’t," I snap. "No sudden moves."

He freezes.

They keep coming.

Smiling.

And all around us, the music rolls.



####

They keep dancing.

I should order suppression fire. Drop smoke. Call command.

Instead, I watch her.

Just a silhouette at first. Backlit by flame. Her hair loose. Her coat too thin. Barefoot, even in this cold. Laughing with someone near the fire. A bottle in her hand. Spinning. Spilling joy like it doesn’t cost a damn thing.

I know. I know it’s her.

The voice from the speaker. The hymn. The Signal break. That was her. It has to be.

Something in me tips.

"Redmond," I say. "Stand down."

He hesitates.

"All of you. Back off. Weapons low. Let them burn it out."

Halver scowls. "Sir, what the fuck is this?"

"It’s a ceasefire," I say. "You want to be the one who ends it?"

They grumble. But they obey.

I climb up the trench wall. Boots sliding. Hands gripping the frozen lip. I haul myself over like I’m not about to betray everything I’ve ever knelt for.

"Where are you going?" Redmond asks.

I dust my gloves off. Adjust my collar.

"To keep the peace," I say. "Maybe dance a little."

The look on his face is halfway between horror and confusion.

"You. Dance."

"Miracles happen."

I walk.

Past the outer barrier. Past the safe zone. Into the stretch of cratered white where no one was supposed to live.

Rebels laugh. Pass flasks. Someone throws a handful of powdered paint into the fire and it flares green. A woman in a knit scarf dances with a man in boots too big. Another holds hands with a child and spins like it’s a Maypole.

Then she sees me.

I don’t wave.

She doesn’t smile.

But her eyes fix on me like she’s reading a name tag I forgot I had on.

She crosses the distance between us in five slow steps.

"That was you?" I ask.

She doesn’t answer right away. Just lifts one eyebrow. Like I’m late to the punchline.

"Singing," I clarify. "The song. That was you."

"Yup. That was me," she says. "Singing that song."

"You could get harvested for that."

She shrugs. "Good thing I ain’t no citizen of your fucked-up country."

I almost smile. Almost.

"So," she says, tilting her head, "you gonna stand there starin' a hole through my head, or you gonna dance?"

"I don’t... really know how."

"Even better."

She grabs my hand and pulls me in.

The fast song is still playing. Something with horns now. Big band energy. Everyone bouncing. Laughing.

I have no rhythm.

She doesn’t care.

We stumble. I step on her foot. She swears. Then grins. Spins me like she’s leading.

"Jesus," I mutter.

"Not tonight, sweetheart. Just Kat."

I blink.

She said it so casually, like I was supposed to know.

She leans in.

"Don’t get used to it."

And then, as if the universe had perfect timing, the track fades.

The next song rolls in like fog.

Percy Sledge.

"When a man loves a woman..."

She doesn’t ask.

She just steps close. Takes my arms. Puts them around her waist. Pulls my body against hers like it belongs there.

We sway.

Slow. Easy. The snow falls thick now, but no one notices. No one cares.

Her breath is warm against my collar.

She smells like woodsmoke and peppermint and danger.

I don’t know where to put my eyes. So I settle on hers.

Big. Brown. Honest. Nothing hidden.

She’s not scared of me. Not even a little.

And I don’t know what that makes me.

I forget the war.

I forget the hymn.

I forget the boy crying in the trench and the Spark monitors and the reason I ever held a gun.

It’s just her.

Just the music.

Just the moment.

We dance.

I don’t want it to end.

She tilts her head. Mouth close.

"You ever been kissed, Captain?"

"Not like this."

"Then shut up."

She kisses me.

Not soft. Not sweet.

It’s fire. And surrender. And every damn thing I never learned how to ask for.

Her hand on my chest.

Mine in her hair.

It could last forever.

But it doesn’t.

Buzzers blare across the canyon.

The ceasefire is ending.

Everything breaks.

She pulls away. Just enough to breathe.

"See you around, Captain."

Then she’s gone.

The fires snuff out.

The Signal dies.

And the world remembers it was supposed to be at war.
